document.title: Evaluation of Two Objective Methods to Optimize kVp and Personnel Exposure Using a Digital Indirect Flat Panel Detector and Simulated Veterinary Patients

sourceSummary.oneLine: Study evaluating objective kVp optimization methods for small animal digital radiography using an indirect flat panel detector and simulated veterinary patients.
sourceSummary.officialUse: Supporting literature for clinical evaluation, radiation exposure considerations, and technique optimization rationale.
keyPoints: The study compared optimization based on signal difference-to-noise ratio alone versus figure of merit incorporating personnel exposure.
A single standard technique of 100 kVp/2.5 mAs was only optimal for most medium-sized patients.
Thinner patients should be imaged with lower kVp, while very large patients require kVp higher than 100.
Personnel exposure from optimized techniques was low and not expected to exceed annual occupational dose limits.

concepts.name: signal difference-to-noise ratio
concepts.evidence: The calculated signal difference-to-noise ratio was used as an objective measure of image quality.

concepts.name: figure of merit
concepts.pageTarget: Figure of Merit in Radiographic Optimization
concepts.evidence: When personnel exposure was considered, a figure of merit was calculated as an endpoint of optimization.

concepts.name: personnel exposure
concepts.evidence: Scattered radiation was recorded and used as a measure of personnel exposure.

concepts.name: ALARA
concepts.evidence: by incorporating the principles of ALARA into technique optimization using an objective endpoint

concepts.name: small animal digital radiography
concepts.evidence: Key words: digital flat panel detector, FOM, figure of merit, kVp, S d NR, signal difference-to-noise ratio, small animal digital radiography, personnel exposure.

evidence.claim: The article evaluated two objective methods for determining optimal kVp values for an indirect flat panel digital detector.
evidence.evidenceText: The purpose of this study was to evaluate two objective methods for determining optimal kVp values for an indirect flat panel digital detector.

evidence.claim: One optimization method considered both image quality and personnel exposure, while the other considered only image quality.
evidence.evidenceText: One method considered both image quality and personnel exposure as endpoints and one considered only image quality.

evidence.claim: The detector studied was the Canon CXDI-50G indirect flat panel detector.
evidence.evidenceText: The detector used in this study was a solid-state, amorphous silicon, indirect, flat panel detector∗. ∗CXDI-50G, Canon, USA, Inc., Lake Success, NY.

evidence.claim: The tested kVp values were 60, 81, 100, and 121.
evidence.evidenceText: The test kVp values were 60, 81, 100, and 121.

evidence.claim: A standard technique of 100 kVp and 2.5 mAs was evaluated.
evidence.evidenceText: patient phantoms were exposed to the test standard technique of 100 kVp and 2.5 mAs

evidence.claim: A single 100 kVp/2.5 mAs standard technique was only optimal for most medium-sized patients.
evidence.evidenceText: Findings indicated that a single standard technique of 100 kVp/2.5 mAs was only optimal for most medium-sized patients.

evidence.claim: Thinner patients should be imaged with lower kVp.
evidence.evidenceText: Images of thinner patients should be made with a lower kVp.

evidence.claim: Very large patients require kVp higher than 100 regardless of optimization method.
evidence.evidenceText: Very large patients require a higher kVp than 100 regardless of the optimization method used.

evidence.claim: Personnel exposure increased when patient thickness and kVp increased.
evidence.evidenceText: personnel exposure increased when both patient thickness and kVp increased.

evidence.claim: Personnel exposure from optimized techniques was low and not expected to exceed annual occupational dose limits.
evidence.evidenceText: Personnel exposure from optimized techniques was low and not expected to exceed annual occupational dose limits.

evidence.claim: The study references an annual occupational dose for adults of 0.5 sievert set by the state Radiation Protection Commission.
evidence.evidenceText: annual occupational dose for adults of 0.5 sievert set forth by the institution’s state Radiation Protection Commission
